quiz_questions.question_text: What is the molecular geometry of methane (CH₄)?

options.text: Tetrahedral
Square planar
Trigonal pyramidal
Linear

quiz_questions.explanation: CH₄ has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs around carbon, giving it a tetrahedral electron geometry and tetrahedral molecular geometry with bond angles of approximately 109.5°.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the bond angle in a trigonal planar molecule?
quiz_questions.explanation: In a trigonal planar arrangement, three groups of electrons spread out evenly in a plane, creating bond angles of 120° (e.g., BF₃).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the molecular shape of water (H₂O)?

options.text: Tetrahedral
Bent
Linear
Trigonal planar

quiz_questions.explanation: Water has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs around oxygen. The electron geometry is tetrahedral, but the molecular geometry (considering only atoms) is bent with a bond angle of about 104.5°.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many electron domains are around the central atom in SF₆?
quiz_questions.explanation: SF₆ has 6 bonding pairs around the central sulfur atom with no lone pairs. This gives 6 electron domains, resulting in an octahedral geometry with 90° bond angles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the molecular geometry of ammonia (NH₃)?

options.text: Tetrahedral
Trigonal pyramidal
Trigonal planar
Bent

quiz_questions.explanation: NH₃ has 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair around nitrogen. The electron geometry is tetrahedral, but the molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal with bond angles of about 107°.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which molecule is nonpolar despite having polar bonds?

options.text: HF
H₂O
CO₂
NH₃

quiz_questions.explanation: CO₂ has two polar C=O bonds, but its linear geometry causes the bond dipoles to point in opposite directions and cancel out, resulting in a net dipole moment of zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What hybridization does the central carbon in ethylene (C₂H₄) exhibit?

options.text: sp³d
sp²
sp
sp³

quiz_questions.explanation: Each carbon in ethylene has 3 electron domains (2 C-H bonds and 1 C=C bond). Three electron domains require sp² hybridization, producing a trigonal planar arrangement with 120° bond angles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the shape of XeF₂?

options.text: Bent
Trigonal planar
Linear
T-shaped

quiz_questions.explanation: XeF₂ has 2 bonding pairs and 3 lone pairs (5 electron domains, trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry). The two fluorines occupy axial positions, giving a linear molecular geometry (180°).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the approximate H-N-H bond angle in ammonia (NH₃)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Ammonia has tetrahedral electron geometry (109.5° ideal), but the lone pair compresses the bond angle to approximately 107° because lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which molecule has a see-saw (sawhorse) shape?

options.text: PCl₅
XeF₄
SF₄
BrF₃

quiz_questions.explanation: SF₄ has 4 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair (5 electron domains, trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry). The lone pair occupies an equatorial position, producing a see-saw molecular shape.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is VSEPR theory?

options.text: A theory that predicts the color of chemical compounds
Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory, which predicts molecular geometry by minimizing repulsion between electron pairs around the central atom
A theory that describes how atomic orbitals combine to form new hybrid orbitals
A theory about electron transfer in ionic bonding

quiz_questions.explanation: VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) theory predicts molecular shapes by arranging electron domains around a central atom to minimize electrostatic repulsion between them.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron domain' mean in VSEPR theory?

options.text: A region of electron density around the central atom, including bonding pairs, lone pairs, and multiple bonds (each counting as one domain)
The total number of electrons in the molecule regardless of prevailing conditions in practice
Only the bonding electron pairs around the central atom under most circumstances involving bonds
The space occupied by core electrons, as conventional bonds theory would suggest

quiz_questions.explanation: An electron domain is any region of electron density around the central atom. Single bonds, double bonds, triple bonds, and lone pairs each count as one domain in VSEPR theory.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'hybridization'?

options.text: The mixing of atomic orbitals on the same atom to form new orbitals of equal energy and shape for bonding
The sharing of electrons between two different elements
The combination of two atoms to form a molecule
The resonance between two Lewis structures

quiz_questions.explanation: Hybridization is the mixing of atomic orbitals (s, p, d) on the same atom to produce new hybrid orbitals (sp, sp², sp³, etc.) that have the same energy and appropriate geometry for bonding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dipole moment' mean?

options.text: The total number of bonds in a molecule
The magnetic property of a molecule with unpaired electrons
The electronegativity difference between two atoms in a bond
A measure of the net polarity of a molecule, calculated as the vector sum of all individual bond dipoles

quiz_questions.explanation: Dipole moment is a vector quantity measuring a molecule's overall polarity. It is the vector sum of all bond dipoles. Symmetric molecules (like CO₂) can have polar bonds but zero net dipole moment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'molecular polarity'?

options.text: Whether a molecule has an overall uneven distribution of electron density, resulting in a net dipole moment
Whether a molecule can form ionic bonds
Whether a molecule contains polar bonds
Whether a molecule can conduct electricity

quiz_questions.explanation: Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and molecular geometry. A molecule is polar if its bond dipoles do not cancel out, resulting in a net dipole moment and uneven charge distribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sp³ hybridization' mean?

options.text: The sharing of three pairs of electrons between two atoms
The mixing of one s and two p orbitals to form three hybrid orbitals
The mixing of one s and three p orbitals to form four equivalent hybrid orbitals with tetrahedral arrangement
The mixing of three s and one p orbital

quiz_questions.explanation: In sp³ hybridization, one s orbital and three p orbitals on the same atom combine to form four equivalent sp³ hybrid orbitals arranged tetrahedrally at 109.5° angles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'electron geometry' versus 'molecular geometry'?

options.text: Electron geometry considers all electron domains (bonding and lone pairs), while molecular geometry considers only the positions of atoms
They are the same thing and always give the same shape
Electron geometry is for ionic compounds, molecular geometry is for covalent compounds
Electron geometry only considers lone pairs, molecular geometry only considers bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron geometry describes the arrangement of all electron domains (bonding + lone pairs) around the central atom. Molecular geometry describes only the arrangement of the atoms, ignoring lone pairs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'trigonal bipyramidal' refer to?

options.text: A geometry with 5 electron domains, having 3 equatorial positions (120° apart) and 2 axial positions (90° from equatorial)
A planar geometry with 3 bonds at 120°
A geometry with 4 electron domains arranged at 109.5°
A geometry with 6 electron domains at 90° angles

quiz_questions.explanation: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry has 5 electron domains: 3 equatorial positions in a plane (120° apart) and 2 axial positions above and below the plane (90° from equatorial positions).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'bond angle compression'?

options.text: The shortening of bond length in a multiple bond
The increase in bond angles when double bonds are present
The decrease in bond angles from ideal values caused by the greater repulsive effect of lone pairs compared to bonding pairs
The distortion of bond angles in a ring structure

quiz_questions.explanation: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs because they are closer to the nucleus. This extra repulsion compresses the bond angles below ideal values (e.g., 107° in NH₃ instead of 109.5°).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'octahedral geometry' mean?

options.text: A geometry found only in ionic crystals
A geometry with 8 electron domains
A geometry with 8 faces and 6 vertices
A geometry with 6 electron domains arranged symmetrically at 90° angles to each other

quiz_questions.explanation: Octahedral geometry has 6 electron domains symmetrically arranged at 90° angles. The name refers to the 8-faced (octahedral) shape formed when the 6 positions are connected. Examples include SF₆.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical researcher is designing a new drug molecule and needs it to be water-soluble. She knows that polar molecules dissolve better in water. She is choosing between two candidate molecules: one with a tetrahedral shape (no lone pairs on the central atom, all identical substituents) and one with a trigonal pyramidal shape. Which candidate is more likely to be water-soluble?

options.text: The trigonal pyramidal molecule, because its asymmetric shape creates a net dipole moment, making it polar
Neither would be water-soluble because organic molecules are always nonpolar
The tetrahedral molecule, because it has more bonds
Both are equally water-soluble because they both have polar bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: A tetrahedral molecule with 4 identical groups is symmetric and nonpolar (dipoles cancel). A trigonal pyramidal molecule has a lone pair creating asymmetry, so its bond dipoles don't cancel, giving it a net dipole moment and polarity.

quiz_questions.question_text: An environmental chemist is studying why carbon dioxide (CO₂) is a greenhouse gas while nitrogen (N₂) is not. Both are atmospheric gases, but only CO₂ absorbs infrared radiation. She considers their molecular geometries. How does CO₂'s geometry allow it to absorb IR radiation during its asymmetric stretching mode?

options.text: During asymmetric stretching, CO₂ temporarily develops a changing dipole moment that can interact with IR radiation, while symmetric N₂ can never develop a dipole
CO₂ is always polar due to its double bonds
CO₂ has a permanent dipole moment that absorbs IR radiation
N₂ absorbs IR radiation equally but reflects it back to space

quiz_questions.explanation: Although CO₂ is linear and nonpolar at equilibrium, its asymmetric stretching and bending vibrations create temporary dipole changes that interact with infrared photons. N₂, being homonuclear and symmetric, cannot develop any dipole change.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemical engineer is designing a refrigerant molecule to replace CFCs. She needs a molecule that has specific boiling point characteristics. She compares two molecules: CCl₄ (tetrahedral, symmetric) and CHCl₃ (tetrahedral but asymmetric). Why does CHCl₃ have a higher boiling point than CCl₄, despite CCl₄ being heavier?

options.text: CHCl₃ has stronger covalent bonds than CCl₄ regardless of prevailing conditions in practice
CHCl₃ has more lone pairs than CCl₄ according to widely held views on this topic
CHCl₃ is polar (asymmetric substitution creates a net dipole), enabling dipole-dipole interactions that CCl₄ lacks due to its symmetry
CCl₄ evaporates faster because it is heavier within the broader context of Chcl analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: CCl₄ is symmetric tetrahedral, so its bond dipoles cancel (nonpolar, only London dispersion forces). CHCl₃ has different substituents (H vs Cl), so dipoles don't cancel, making it polar with additional dipole-dipole interactions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biochemist studying enzyme active sites notices that the nitrogen in an amino group (-NH₂) attached to a protein has a trigonal pyramidal geometry. A student suggests it should be trigonal planar because nitrogen has 3 bonds. Why is the student incorrect?

options.text: The protein forces the nitrogen into a pyramidal shape through steric effects
Nitrogen in -NH₂ has 3 bonding pairs plus 1 lone pair (4 electron domains), giving tetrahedral electron geometry and trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry
Nitrogen always has 4 bonds in organic molecules
Nitrogen uses sp hybridization, which is always pyramidal

quiz_questions.explanation: VSEPR considers all electron domains, not just bonds. Nitrogen in -NH₂ has 3 bonds + 1 lone pair = 4 electron domains (sp³). The lone pair makes the molecular geometry trigonal pyramidal rather than trigonal planar.

quiz_questions.question_text: A materials scientist is studying why xenon tetrafluoride (XeF₄) is perfectly square planar rather than tetrahedral, even though both have 4 bonds to the central atom. What causes this difference in geometry?

options.text: XeF₄ has 6 electron domains (4 bonding + 2 lone pairs) with octahedral electron geometry; the 2 lone pairs occupy opposite axial positions, creating a square planar molecular shape
Xenon is a noble gas and follows different geometry rules regardless of prevailing conditions in practice
Fluorine atoms repel each other into a square arrangement, despite what intuition might initially suggest
XeF₄ has sp³ hybridization like CH₄, since standard models of pairs behavior predict this outcome under normal conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: XeF₄ has 4 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs (6 electron domains = octahedral electron geometry). The 2 lone pairs occupy positions 180° apart (trans) to minimize repulsion, leaving the 4 F atoms in a square planar arrangement.

quiz_questions.question_text: A climate scientist is analyzing the greenhouse gas sulfur hexafluoride (SF₆), which has a global warming potential 23,500 times that of CO₂. Despite having very polar S-F bonds, SF₆ is a nonpolar molecule. How does its geometry explain this paradox?

options.text: SF₆ has ionic bonds that cancel each other out
The lone pairs on fluorine cancel the bond dipoles
Sulfur is not electronegative enough to create polar bonds with fluorine
SF₆ has octahedral geometry where the 6 S-F bond dipoles are arranged symmetrically and cancel out completely, giving zero net dipole moment

quiz_questions.explanation: SF₆'s octahedral geometry places 6 polar S-F bonds in perfectly symmetric positions. Each bond dipole has an equal and opposite partner, causing all dipoles to cancel. The molecule has no net dipole moment despite very polar individual bonds.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is building molecular models and notices that the C-C-C bond angle in cyclopropane (a 3-carbon ring) is forced to be 60°, much less than the ideal tetrahedral angle of 109.5°. What does this tell the student about cyclopropane's stability compared to cyclohexane (6-carbon ring with ~109.5° angles)?

options.text: Cyclopropane is more stable because smaller rings have stronger bonds
Cyclopropane has significant angle strain because its bond angles deviate greatly from ideal sp³ angles, making it less stable and more reactive than cyclohexane
Cyclopropane is more stable because 60° angles allow better orbital overlap
Both rings have the same stability because they both contain sp³ carbons

quiz_questions.explanation: The 60° bond angles in cyclopropane deviate significantly from the ideal sp³ angle of 109.5°, creating substantial angle strain. This stored energy makes cyclopropane reactive and less stable than strain-free cyclohexane.

quiz_questions.question_text: A food chemist is studying the flavor molecule vanillin. She determines that the carbon bonded to the oxygen in the aldehyde group (C=O) has a trigonal planar geometry. What hybridization must this carbon have, and why?

options.text: sp, because the carbon forms a double bond, as the dynamics involved tend to push conditions in this direction over time
sp², because the carbon has 3 electron domains (double bond to O, single bond to C, single bond to H), requiring 3 hybrid orbitals in a trigonal planar arrangement
sp³, because carbon always forms 4 bonds, since the prevailing theory about bond would support this particular interpretation
sp³d, because the double bond uses d orbitals, as most introductory frameworks would predict when analyzing bond dynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: The aldehyde carbon has 3 electron domains: a C=O double bond (counts as 1 domain), a C-C bond, and a C-H bond. Three electron domains require sp² hybridization with 120° angles and trigonal planar geometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: A toxicologist compares the structures of cis-platin (a cancer drug) and trans-platin (inactive form). Both have the formula PtCl₂(NH₃)₂ with square planar geometry around platinum. In cis-platin, the two Cl atoms are adjacent (90° apart), while in trans-platin they are opposite (180° apart). Why is cis-platin active as a drug while trans-platin is not?

options.text: Trans-platin is too polar to enter cancer cells because the underlying forces at work tend to produce exactly this type of outcome
The adjacent arrangement of Cl atoms in cis-platin allows both to interact with and crosslink DNA strands, while the opposite arrangement in trans-platin cannot achieve this crosslinking
Cis-platin has stronger Pt-Cl bonds, which is consistent with how platin mechanisms are understood to operate in theory
Cis-platin has a higher molecular weight than trans-platin when the interaction between platin and trans factors is taken into full account

quiz_questions.explanation: In cis-platin, the two adjacent Cl ligands can be replaced by nitrogen atoms on the same DNA strand, creating intrastrand crosslinks that prevent DNA replication. Trans-platin's geometry doesn't allow effective DNA crosslinking.

quiz_questions.question_text: A polymer chemist is working with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE, Teflon). Each carbon in the polymer chain is bonded to two fluorines and two other carbons. She wants to determine if the C-F bonds create a polar or nonpolar polymer. Considering that each carbon is sp³ hybridized with tetrahedral geometry and identical substituent pattern along the chain, is PTFE polar or nonpolar?

options.text: Polar, because fluorine has the highest electronegativity
Effectively nonpolar overall, because the symmetric arrangement of F atoms around each tetrahedral carbon causes bond dipoles to largely cancel along the polymer chain
Nonpolar, because fluorine-fluorine interactions cancel out all polarity
Polar, because C-F bonds are among the most polar covalent bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Although individual C-F bonds are very polar, the symmetric -CF₂- arrangement along the chain causes the dipoles to approximately cancel. This makes PTFE effectively nonpolar, explaining its nonstick, hydrophobic properties.
